title: The Millennium Falcon started off with a different design
content: The Millennium Falcon was almost unrecognizable in its first design, a long, tube-like ship that model makers felt replicated the one seen in Space: 1999. George Lucas scrapped it and issued a new challenge: make something totally unique, like a flying hamburger with the grace of a sunfish. The original concept didn't go to waste though. It became the Rebel Blockade Runner, the ship desperately escaping the Imperial Star Destroyer in A New Hope's opening scene.

title: Lucas used war footage for inspiration
content: Lucas wanted Star Wars' space battles to feel real, so he turned to actual war footage. Back in the late 1970s, Industrial Light & Magic (ILM) was a scrappy team working out of a bare-bones warehouse. To guide them, Lucas spliced together old World War II dogfight reels, and ILM followed his lead, recreating some shots frame by frame, including the Millennium Falcon's showdown with the TIE fighters.

title: The Star Wars Holiday Special was a flop
content: The Star Wars Holiday Special is legendary for all the wrong reasons. When brainstorming the 1978 variety show, co-writer Leonard Ripps recalled Lucas mapping it out. His vision was a Wookiee holiday, something between Rosh Hashanah and Earth Day, but furrier.

title: The Force Awakens set a new standard
content: The Force Awakens introduced a new generation of fans to the Star Wars universe. It was a critical and commercial success, proving that the franchise could still captivate audiences decades after the original trilogy.

title: Lucas funded his own projects
content: The Empire Strikes Back exists the way it does because Lucas bet on himself. After Star Wars became a massive hit, the studio still tried to control him, so he funded the sequel himself. It gave him creative freedom, but when the budget spiraled $10 million over, his bank-backed loan fell through. Lucas had no choice but to turn to 20th Century Fox, sacrificing some rights in the process. Frustrated, he took his next big idea, Raiders of the Lost Ark, to rival studio Paramount.

title: A Cheers actor had a role in the Star Wars franchise
content: You might remember actor John Ratzenberger for his role as Cliff Clavin from the TV show Cheers. Before that, Ratzenberger had a small role in The Empire Strikes Back as Major Bren Derlin.

title: The Star Wars cast had an unimpressive wardrobe
content: For its time, Star Wars: A New Hope was an impressive feat of special effects. Apparently, this didn't apply to the cast's wardrobe. Mark Hamill revealed that the pants he wore were simply bleached Levi's blue jeans with the back pockets removed. George Lucas called Star Wars the most expensive low-budget movie ever made, and every penny spent had to show up on the screen.

title: The droid wasn't really a robot
content: There is an actual human being inside the C-3PO costume: actor Anthony Daniels. The costume was so restrictive that Daniels could not sit down while wearing it. During scenes where C-3PO was seated, Daniels often had to be positioned first and then have parts of the costume assembled around him.

title: The Star Wars cast had to work with the wind
content: During filming of Empire Strikes Back, wind machines were so loud that Mark Hamill could not hear Darth Vader's lines. Hamill later explained that he relied on visual cues to know when to respond, comparing the experience to acting in a silent movie, except much louder.

title: There was an issue with one of the Star Wars props
content: In Empire Strikes Back, a prop head of Mark Hamill was made for a scene where Luke sees himself in Darth Vader's helmet. Hamill said the prop was unsatisfactory, so they re-shot the scene using his real head pushed through an opening below the set.

title: Blue Milk wasn't the tastiest
content: The famous blue milk seen in Star Wars was made from long-life milk mixed with blue food coloring. According to Mark Hamill, it was oily, warm and slightly sweet, and drinking it on camera was an acting challenge.

title: Skywalker's vehicle was called a Podracer
content: In The Phantom Menace, Anakin Skywalker races a Podracer. One of the vehicles used in production was constructed from the shell of a Maserati Birdcage, a racing car first introduced in the 1960s.

title: There were plenty of unscripted moments on Star Wars
content: A scene in The Last Jedi where Luke kisses Leia on the forehead was not in the script. Mark Hamill described it as a spontaneous moment that summarized his feelings and Luke's feelings in a way words could not.

title: The Star Wars fighter pilot suits had an unexpected issue
content: During filming of A New Hope in England, a record heatwave made fighter pilot costumes uncomfortable. Many actors wore only the upper half of their costumes on camera and shorts below the waist.

title: Star Wars was originally written with a female lead
content: In an early 1975 draft of what would become Star Wars: A New Hope, George Lucas rewrote his main character, Luke Starkiller, as an 18-year-old girl in response to the lack of female characters. Later drafts reverted the character back to a boy.
